revision.comment: moved [[Suzaku, (Kyoto)]] to [[Suzaku Avenue]]: easier to spell, easier to find, more common name

revision.text:
In ancient Japan, imperial capitals were designed in a grid pattern according to the Chinese traditional city plan. '''Suzaku Avenue''', or '''Suzaku Boulevard''', was the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial palace from the south. As per the Chinese tradition, the palace complex usually faces south, the Suzaku Avenue thus leads straight up to the main palace gate, making it generally the widest street in the capital.
The word "[[Suzaku]]", refers to the guardian god of the south, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
[[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan]]
{{Japan-struct-stub}}

In ancient Japan, imperial capitals were designed in a grid pattern according to the Chinese traditional city plan. '''Suzaku Avenue''', or '''Suzaku Boulevard''', was the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial palace from the south. As per the Chinese tradition, the palace complex usually faces south, the Suzaku Avenue thus leads straight up to the main palace gate, making it generally the widest street in the capital.
The word "[[Suzaku]]", refers to the guardian god of the south, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
[[ja:朱雀大路]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan]]
{{Japan-struct-stub}}

[[Image:Heiankyo palace location.png|250px|thumb|Heian-kyō. '''Suzaku Avenue''' shown in bold vertical line]]
{{Nihongo|'''Suzaku Avenue''' or '''Suzaku Boulevard'''|朱雀大路|Suzaku Ōji}} was a street in ancient [[Japan]]ese capital. In the center of grid pattern planned according to the Chinese tradition, it was the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial palace from the south. As per the Chinese tradition, the palace complex usually faces south, the Suzaku Avenue thus leads straight up to the main palace gate, making it generally the widest street in the capital. [[Fujiwara-kyō]], [[Heijō-kyō]], [[Heian-kyō]] had their own Suzaku Avenue.
==Route== In [[Heian-kyō]], present [[Kyoto]], south end was the [[Rashōmon]] (Rajōmon, Raseimon) and northern end was the main gate of the [[Heian Palace]]. This street became, however, off the city center soon, due to abandonment of west part of the city. Until [[Meiji period]], the street was the west boundary of the town. Presently it is still a major street named {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for [[tram]].
The word "[[Suzaku]]", refers to the guardian god of the south, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
[[ja:朱雀大路]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Japan]] [[Category:Kyoto]]
{{Japan-struct-stub}}

[[Image:Heiankyo palace location.png|250px|thumb|Heian-kyō. '''Suzaku Avenue''' shown in bold vertical line]]
{{Nihongo|'''Suzaku Avenue''' or '''Suzaku Boulevard'''|朱雀大路|Suzaku Ōji}} was a street in ancient [[Japan]]ese capital. In the center of grid pattern planned according to the Chinese tradition, it was the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial palace from the south. As per the Chinese tradition, the palace complex usually faces south, the Suzaku Avenue thus leads straight up to the main palace gate, making it generally the widest street in the capital. [[Fujiwara-kyō]], [[Heijō-kyō]], [[Heian-kyō]] had their own Suzaku Avenue.
==Route== In [[Heian-kyō]], present [[Kyoto]], south end was the [[Rashōmon]] (Rajōmon, Raseimon) and northern end was the main gate of the [[Heian Palace]]. This street became, however, off the city center soon, due to abandonment of west part of the city. Until [[Meiji period]], the street was the west boundary of the town. Presently it is still a major street named {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for [[tram]].
The word "[[Suzaku]]", refers to the guardian god of the south, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|auto=yes|date=December 2009}}
[[File:Heiankyo palace location.png|250px|thumb|Heian-kyō. '''Suzaku Avenue''' shown in bold vertical line]] [[File:SuzakuOji street of Heiankyo.jpg|thumb|220px|A reconstructed model of Suzaku Avenue and Rashōmon exhibited at Heiankyo Sosei-Kan Museum]] {{Nihongo|'''Suzaku Avenue''' or '''Suzaku Boulevard'''|朱雀大路|Suzaku Ōji}} is the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial Palace from the south in [[Japan]]ese capitals. Traditionally the Imperial palace complex faces south, whilst Suzaku Avenue leads directly away from the main gate. Cities were often based on a traditional Chinese grid pattern. Suzaku Avenue was typically the central road within the city grid, and as a result, the widest. [[Fujiwara-kyō]], [[Heijō-kyō]], and [[Heian-kyō]] had their own Suzaku Avenue.
The word "[[Vermilion Bird|Suzaku]]" refers to the Guardian God of the South, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
==Heian-kyō== In Heian-kyō, present-day [[Kyoto]], the [[Rajōmon]] (Rajōmon, Raseimon) was at the southern end of Suzaku Avenue, flanked on the east by the temple of [[Tō-ji]], and on the west by the temple of [[Sai-ji]], whilst at the northern end there was the main gate [[Suzakumon]] of [[Heian Palace]]. Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the [[Meiji period]]. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's [[tram]]line.
==Fujiwara-kyō==
==Heijō-kyō==

'''Suzaku Avenue''' shown in bold vertical line]] [[File:SuzakuOji street of Heiankyo.jpg|thumb|220px|A reconstructed model of Suzaku Avenue and Rashōmon exhibited at Heiankyo Sosei-Kan Museum]] {{Nihongo|'''Suzaku Avenue''' or '''Suzaku Boulevard'''|朱雀大路|Suzaku Ōji}} is the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial Palace from the south in [[Japan]]ese capitals. Traditionally the Imperial palace complex faces south, whilst Suzaku Avenue leads directly away from the main gate. Cities were often based on a traditional Chinese grid pattern. Suzaku Avenue was typically the central road within the city grid, and as a result, the widest. [[Fujiwara-kyō]], [[Heijō-kyō]], and [[Heian-kyō]] had their own Suzaku Avenue.
The word "[[Vermilion Bird|Suzaku]]" refers to the Guardian God of the South, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
==Heian-kyō== In Heian-kyō, present-day [[Kyoto]], the [[Rajōmon]] (Rajōmon, Raseimon) was at the southern end of Suzaku Avenue, flanked on the east by the temple of [[Tō-ji]], and on the west by the temple of [[Sai-ji]], whilst at the northern end there was the main gate [[Suzakumon]] of [[Heian Palace]]. Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the [[Meiji period]]. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's [[tram]]line.

'''Suzaku Avenue''' shown in bold vertical line]] [[File:SuzakuOji street of Heiankyo.jpg|thumb|220px|A reconstructed model of Suzaku Avenue and Rashōmon exhibited at Heiankyo Sosei-Kan Museum]] {{Nihongo|'''Suzaku Avenue''' or '''Suzaku Boulevard'''|朱雀大路|Suzaku Ōji}} is the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial Palace from the south in [[Japan]]ese capitals. Traditionally the Imperial palace complex faces south, whilst Suzaku Avenue leads directly away from the main gate. Cities were often based on a traditional Chinese grid pattern. Suzaku Avenue was typically the central road within the city grid, and as a result, the widest. [[Fujiwara-kyō]], [[Heijō-kyō]], and [[Heian-kyō]] had their own Suzaku Avenue.
The word "[[Vermilion Bird|Suzaku]]" refers to the Guardian God of the South, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
==Heian-kyō== In Heian-kyō, present-day [[Kyoto]], the [[Rajōmon]] (Rajōmon, Raseimon) was at the southern end of Suzaku Avenue, flanked on the east by the temple of [[Tō-ji]], and on the west by the temple of [[Sai-ji]], whilst at the northern end there was the main gate [[Suzakumon]] of [[Heian Palace]]. Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the [[Meiji period]]. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's [[tram]]line.

Suzaku Avenue was typically the central road within the city grid, and as a result, the widest. [[Fujiwara-kyō]], [[Heijō-kyō]], and [[Heian-kyō]] had their own Suzaku Avenue.
The word "[[Vermilion Bird|Suzaku]]" refers to the Guardian God of the South, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
==Heian-kyō== In Heian-kyō, present-day [[Kyoto]], the [[Rajōmon]] (Rajōmon, Raseimon) was at the southern end of Suzaku Avenue, flanked on the east by the temple of [[Tō-ji]], and on the west by the temple of [[Sai-ji]], whilst at the northern end there was the main gate [[Suzakumon]] of [[Heian Palace]]. Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the [[Meiji period]]. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's [[tram]]line.

'''Suzaku Avenue''' shown in bold vertical line]] [[File:SuzakuOji street of Heiankyo.jpg|thumb|220px|A reconstructed model of Suzaku Avenue and Rashōmon exhibited at Heiankyo Sosei-Kan Museum]] {{Nihongo|'''Suzaku Avenue''' or '''Suzaku Boulevard'''|朱雀大路|Suzaku Ōji}} is the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial Palace from the south in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese capitals. Traditionally the Imperial palace complex faces south, whilst Suzaku Avenue leads directly away from the main gate. Cities were often based on a traditional Chinese grid pattern. Suzaku Avenue was typically the central road within the city grid, and as a result, the widest. [[:en:Fujiwara-kyō|Fujiwara-kyō]], [[Heijō-kyō]], and [[:en:Heian-kyō|Heian-kyō]] had their own Suzaku Avenue.
The word "[[:en:Vermilion Bird|Suzaku]]" refers to the Guardian God of the South, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
==Heian-kyō== In Heian-kyō, present-day [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], the [[:en:Rajōmon|Rajōmon]] (Rajōmon, Raseimon) was at the southern end of Suzaku Avenue, flanked on the east by the temple of [[Tō-ji]], and on the west by the temple of [[:en:Sai-ji|Sai-ji]], whilst at the northern end there was the main gate [[:en:Suzakumon|Suzakumon]] of [[:en:Heian Palace|Heian Palace]]. Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]]. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's [[:en:tram|tram]]line.
==Fujiwara-kyō==
==Heijō-kyō==

Traditionally the Imperial palace complex faces south, whilst Suzaku Avenue leads directly away from the main gate. Cities were often based on a traditional Chinese grid pattern. Suzaku Avenue was typically the central road within the city grid, and as a result, the widest. {{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|WD=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō}}, [[Heijō-kyō]], and {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} had their own Suzaku Avenue.
The word "{{ill|Vermilion Bird|en|Vermilion Bird|WD=Q1059392|lt=Suzaku}}" refers to the Guardian God of the South, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.

Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's {{ill|tram|en|tram|WD=Q3407658|lt=tram}}line.

'''Suzaku Avenue''' shown in bold vertical line]] [[File:SuzakuOji street of Heiankyo.jpg|thumb|220px|A reconstructed model of Suzaku Avenue and Rashōmon exhibited at Heiankyo Sosei-Kan Museum]] {{Nihongo|'''Suzaku Avenue''' or '''Suzaku Boulevard'''|朱雀大路|Suzaku Ōji}} is the name given to the central avenue leading to the Imperial Palace from the south in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese capitals. Traditionally the Imperial palace complex faces south, whilst Suzaku Avenue leads directly away from the main gate. Cities were often based on a traditional Chinese grid pattern. Suzaku Avenue was typically the central road within the city grid, and as a result, the widest. {{ill|Fujiwara-kyō|en|Fujiwara-kyō|WD=Q1196381|lt=Fujiwara-kyō}}, [[Heijō-kyō]], and {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|WD=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō}} had their own Suzaku Avenue.
The word "{{ill|Vermilion Bird|en|Vermilion Bird|WD=Q1059392|lt=Suzaku}}" refers to the Guardian God of the South, who was said to appear in the form of a bird.
==Heian-kyō== In Heian-kyō, present-day {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, the {{ill|Rajōmon|en|Rajōmon|WD=Q1344338|lt=Rajōmon}} (Rajōmon, Raseimon) was at the southern end of Suzaku Avenue, flanked on the east by the temple of [[Tō-ji]], and on the west by the temple of {{ill|Sai-ji|en|Sai-ji|WD=Q135714|lt=Sai-ji}}, whilst at the northern end there was the main gate {{ill|Suzakumon|en|Suzakumon|WD=Q211614|lt=Suzakumon}} of {{ill|Heian Palace|en|Heian Palace|WD=Q304501|lt=Heian Palace}}. Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's {{ill|tram|en|tram|WD=Q3407658|lt=tram}}line.
==Fujiwara-kyō==
==Heijō-kyō==

==Heian-kyō== In Heian-kyō, present-day {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, the {{ill|Rajōmon|en|Rajōmon|WD=Q1344338|lt=Rajōmon|1=Rajōmon}} (Rajōmon, Raseimon) was at the southern end of Suzaku Avenue, flanked on the east by the temple of [[Tō-ji]], and on the west by the temple of {{ill|Sai-ji|en|Sai-ji|WD=Q135714|lt=Sai-ji|1=Sai-ji}}, whilst at the northern end there was the main gate {{ill|Suzakumon|en|Suzakumon|WD=Q211614|lt=Suzakumon|1=Suzakumon}} of {{ill|Heian Palace|en|Heian Palace|WD=Q304501|lt=Heian Palace|1=Heian Palace}}. Of these, only Tō-ji remains.
Over time Suzaku Avenue stopped being the central street, due to the gradual abandonment of the west of the city. Eventually the road served as the western boundary of the town, until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. Presently it is still a major street, called {{Nihongo|Senbon Street or Senbon Avenue|千本通|Senbon Dōri}}, and once served as a route for the city's {{ill|tram|en|tram|WD=Q3407658|lt=tram|1=Tram}}line.
